UNCONTESTED CALENDAR OF JUL-26-2022 NOTICE OF HEARING ON PLAINTIFFS ALEJANDRO VELARDE AND BECKY SCHWEIGHARDTS REQUEST FOR DEFAULT JUDGMENT - CONTINUED TO SEP-29-2022 AT 9:00 AM IN DEPT. 514 FOR FURTHER DEFAULT PROVE-UP HEARING, AND IN ORDER FOR COUNSEL TO BRIEF THE ISSUE OF PLAINTIFFS' NON-COMPLAIANCE WITH THE NOTICE REQUIREMENT RE: REQUESTED DAMAGES IN THE COMPLAINT. ADDITIONAL BRIEFING SHALL BE E-FILED NO LATER THAN 09/29/2022, WITH A COURTESY COPY EMAILED TO THE CLERK IN DEPARTMENT 514 THAT SAME DAY. RON LUNSKI, ESQ. APPEARED WITH PLAINTIFFS ALEJANDRO VELARDE AND BECKY SCHWEIGHARDT, WHO WERE PLACED UNDER OATH, BUT DID NOT TESTIFY. MESSRS. LUNSKI AND VELARDE, ALONG WITH MS. SCHWEIGHARDT, APPEARED VIA ZOOM FOR THIS MORNING'S PROCEEDINGS. A. THOMAS KOSTER, ESQ. APPEARED WITH DEFENDANT LUKE O'BRIEN, NEITHER OF WHOM PARTICIPATED IN TODAY'S HEARING. PLAINTIFFS TO PROVIDE NOTICE OF THE CONTINUED HEARING DATE. PROCEEDINGS RECORDED BY "FOR THE RECORD" COMMENCING AT 10:40 A.M., ADJOURNING AT 10:46 A.M., RECONVENING AT 10:48 A.M. AND CONCLUDING AT 10:49 A.M. JUDGE: MURLENE J. RANDLE. CLERK: K. DOUGHERTY. (D514)

Notice Of Hearing On Plaintiffs Alejandro Velarde And Becky Schweighardts Request For Default Judgment
Off Calendar Sep-29-2022 Off Calendar

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Sep-01-2022 Continued To Oct-19-22 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

DEFENDANTS LUKE O'BRIEN AND VSA CONSTRUCTION, INC.'S MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT
Off Calendar Aug-31-2022 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. ANDREW C. MCCLELLAND, Esq. of Lunski & Stimpert, LLP (Tel: 415-426-3521) for plaintiffs;
Atty. A. THOMAS KOSTER, Esq. of Koster & Leadbetter LLP (Tel: 415-713-8680) for defendants Luke O'Brien and VSA Construction, Inc.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANTS LUKE O'BRIEN AND VSA CONSTRUCTION, INC.'S MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling with a modification, as follows:

Defendants' (LUKE O'BRIEN and VSA CONSTRUCTION, INC.) motion to vacate the defaults entered on October 29, 2021, is granted. There is a liberal policy to hear cases on the merits. (See Fasuyi v. Permatex, Inc. (2008) 167 Cal.App.4th 681, 685.)

Defendants seek relief from the defaults based on the court's equitable powers. For equitable relief, the defaulted party must demonstrate that it has: (1) a meritorious case, (2) a satisfactory excuse for not presenting a defense to the original action, and (3) diligence in seeking to set aside the default once the extrinsic fraud or mistake had been discovered. (See Mechling v. Asbestos Defendants (2018) 29 Cal.App.5th 1241, 1246.)

Defendants demonstrate a meritorious case/defense. "It has long been established that merely attaching a verification to a proposed answer is sufficient to demonstrate meritoriousness". (Stiles v. Wallis (1983) 147 Cal.App.3d 1143, 1148.) Here, defendants provide an unverified proposed answer, but the reply correctly notes that alter ego liability is difficult to establish and the San Francisco Department of Building Inspection approved the construction, which indicates that there were no code violations. (See RJN Ex. 2; Koster Decl., Ex. 5.) Defendants "show facts indicating a sufficiently meritorious claim to entitle [them] to a fair adversary hearing." (In re marriage of Park (1980) 27 Cal.3d 337, 346.) "[O]nly a minimal showing is necessary." (Mechling, 29 Cal.App.5th at 1246.)

Defendants present a satisfactory excuse for not presenting a defense. Defendants tendered the claim to their insurers and reasonably believed that they were working with plaintiff to resolve the claim. (See O'Brien Decl.) In fact, one of the insurers requested extra time to respond to the complaint and was communicating with plaintiff's counsel when plaintiff went ahead and obtained the defaults. (O'Brien Decl., Ex. 1 [e-mail chain].) "The law looks with disfavor upon a party who, regardless of the merits of his case, attempts to take advantage of the mistake, surprise, inadvertence, or neglect of his adversary.'

Defendants have been diligent. The last insurer denied the claim on March 17, 2022, and defendants attempted to have the insurers reconsider their decisions. (O'Brien Decl., pars. 18-23.) When defendants received the default judgment documents in May of 2022, they retained counsel. (O'Brien Decl., par. 25.) Counsel explains that it took some time to file this motion because co-counsel was out of the country, counsel's father passed away, and counsel needed to investigate the case and he attempted to have the insurers reconsider their denials. (Koster Decl., pars. 5-6.)

The court therefore finds good cause to grant defendants the requested relief.

Prevailing party to prepare and submit a proposed order which complies with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported. (302/RBU)
